title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the Grafana dashboard showing a consistent memory climb from ~500MB to 4GB+ is very helpful and strongly suggests a memory leak.

Could you share your environment details? Specifically:
- What version of TaskFlow are you running?
- What OS and runtime version is the server running on (e.g., Node.js version, Java version)?
- What database are you using (type and version)?
- How is TaskFlow deployed (bare metal, Docker, Kubernetes, etc.)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We're running TaskFlow v2.3 — we upgraded from v2.2 about a week ago, which is actually right around when this problem started, now that I think about it. The server is on an Ubuntu 22.04 VM with 8GB RAM. It's a bare metal deployment, not containerized. I'm not sure offhand what the exact runtime version is, but I can check if that's important. We're using PostgreSQL for the database, though I'd have to look up the exact version.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the additional context — the timing of the v2.2 → v2.3 upgrade lining up with the start of this issue is a really important clue.

Do you see any error messages or warnings in the TaskFlow server logs, particularly as performance starts to degrade in the afternoon? For example, out-of-memory warnings, database connection pool exhaustion, or any recurring errors that increase in frequency over the day. Even a snippet from the logs around the time things get slow would be very helpful.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I did check the logs last time it happened. There aren't any explicit out-of-memory errors until things get really bad, but I do see a lot of warnings about slow responses and request timeouts that start appearing a few hours into the day and get more frequent as time goes on. I also noticed there are a lot of log entries related to the WebSocket notification system — things about new connections and notification dispatches. The volume of those messages seems higher than I'd expect, but I'm not sure if that's normal for v2.3 since the changelog mentioned 'improved real-time notifications' as a new feature. No database connection pool errors that I've seen though — PostgreSQL seems fine on its end.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak causing progressive server slowdown, likely introduced in v2.3 WebSocket notification system","problem":"Self-hosted TaskFlow instance experiences a consistent daily memory leak, climbing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. This causes progressive performance degradation — page loads exceed 10 seconds and API calls time out, requiring a daily server restart. Affects ~200 active users.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 upgrade introduced an 'improved real-time notifications' feature using WebSockets. The reporter observes an unexpectedly high volume of WebSocket connection and notification dispatch log entries. The most likely cause is that WebSocket connections or their associated resources (event listeners, notification subscriptions, buffers) are not being properly cleaned up when clients disconnect, leading to a cumulative memory leak proportional to connection churn throughout the day.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with monitoring (e.g., Grafana tracking memory usage)","Allow normal user activity (~200 users) over the course of a workday","Observe server memory climbing steadily from baseline (~500MB) toward 4GB+","Observe increasing slow-response warnings and request timeouts in server logs","Note the high volume of WebSocket-related log entries (connections, notification dispatches)"],"environment":"TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 ~1 week ago), Ubuntu 22.04 VM, 8GB RAM, bare metal deployment, PostgreSQL database","severity":"high","impact":"All ~200 active users experience degraded performance daily, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires manual daily restart as a workaround.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the WebSocket notification system introduced/changed in v2.3. Specifically: (1) Check whether WebSocket connections are properly closed and their resources released on client disconnect, navigation, or timeout. (2) Look for event listener or subscription leaks where notification handlers accumulate without cleanup. (3) Compare the WebSocket connection management code between v2.2 and v2.3. (4) Use heap snapshots or memory profiling to confirm whether WebSocket-related objects are the source of the leak.","proposed_test_case":"Create a load test that simulates repeated WebSocket connect/disconnect cycles (mimicking users opening and closing the app throughout the day). Monitor server memory over an extended period. Memory should remain stable after connections are closed. Additionally, a unit test should verify that all event listeners and subscriptions are removed when a WebSocket connection is terminated.","information_gaps":["Exact runtime version (e.g., Node.js version) — unlikely to change the investigation direction but could matter if the leak is runtime-specific","Exact PostgreSQL version — low relevance since DB connection pooling appears healthy","Whether the issue occurs with fewer users or requires the full 200-user load to manifest"]}
